narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 54.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was the key reason your application was rejected—they use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, a middle-income family background, an LSAT score of 26, an undergraduate GPA of 3.30, identifying as female and Hispanic, and planning to attend full-time. Compared to typical students, your school deciles are much lower than the average around the fifth decile, your LSAT is well below the usual 36.7, and while your undergrad GPA is slightly stronger than the typical 3.21, these factors together tipped the scales. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your third-year school decile, which pushed the prediction toward failure because it was so low relative to peers. Next in importance was your LSAT score, similarly driving the risk higher due to its below-average level. Your first-year school decile ranked third in influence, again contributing to the failure prediction for the same reason. Your middle family income followed, playing a moderate role in heightening the perceived risk. Your undergraduate GPA, race as Hispanic, and full-time status had smaller but still notable influences toward failure, while your gender had the least impact. Overall, your academic rankings in school and low LSAT stood out as the top concerns when stacked against what succeeds for most students. The good news is some of these are within your control—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that's a major lever for future applications. Strengthening your academic performance through additional coursework or targeted prep could also help lift those low deciles in any updated records. While background factors like family income or demographics aren't changeable, focusing on these academics has turned predictions around for many in similar spots. Reach out to advisors for bar prep resources early, and reapply after showing improvement—you've already got a solid undergrad GPA as a foundation. Keep pushing; many students overcome these hurdles with persistence.
